name: Erymanthian Boar
primary_sources: Diodorus of Sicily, Library of History
Lucretius, Of The Nature of Things
Philippus of Thessalonica, The Twelve Labors of Hercules
Plutarch, Moralia, On the Fortune of Alexander
Tzetzes, Chiliades or Book of Histories

visual_attributes.morphology: large, robust boar with a massive body
visual_attributes.size_and_scale: gigantic, larger than a typical boar
visual_attributes.structural_arrangement: massive head with prominent tusks, sturdy legs

domain_specific_visual.environment: glens of Lampeia, marsh of Erymanthus

functional_attributes.primary_function: to be captured as part of Heracles' fourth labor
secondary_functions: symbol of wildness and untamed nature

domain_specific_functional.role_in_myth: test of Heracles' strength and cunning

relationships.description: Heracles captured the Erymanthian Boar as part of his fourth labor.
Eurystheus was terrified of the boar and hid himself upon seeing it.

symbolic_items: tusks preserved in the sanctuary of Apollo at Cumae

contextual_attributes.cultural_significance: represents the untamed and dangerous aspects of nature
primary_sources: Diodorus of Sicily, Library of History
Lucretius, Of The Nature of Things
Philippus of Thessalonica, The Twelve Labors of Hercules
Plutarch, Moralia, On the Fortune of Alexander
Tzetzes, Chiliades or Book of Histories

contrastive_constraints: NOT a domesticated or tame animal
